You
now it works. now, how to send different message when password is too short? password must be at least 6 characters long
ChatGPT

To handle different messages for different validation scenarios (such as a short password), you can modify your backend route and frontend component accordingly.

Backend (usersRouter.post):

javascript
usersRouter.post('/', async (request, response) => {
    try {
        const body = request.body;

        if (body.password.length < 6) {
            return response.status(400).json({ error: 'Password is too short (minimum 6 characters).' });
        }

        const saltRounds = 10;
        const passwordHash = await bcrypt.hash(body.password, saltRounds);

        const user = new User({
            name: body.name,
            username: body.username,
            passwordHash,
            email: body.email
        });
        const savedUser = await user.save();

        response.status(201).json(savedUser);
    } catch (error) {
        console.error(error);
        response.status(400).json({ error: 'All fields not filled.' });
    }
});

Frontend (Register):

javascript
const handleRegister = async (event) => {
    event.preventDefault();
    try {
        const newUser = await userService.register({
            username, password, email
        });
        setUsername('');
        setPassword('');
        setEmail('');
        window.localStorage.setItem('loggedUser', JSON.stringify(newUser));
        pictureService.setToken(newUser.token);
        navigate('/');
    } catch (exception) {
        console.error(exception);

        if (exception.response && exception.response.status === 400) {
            dispatch(setNotification(exception.response.data.error));
        } else {
            dispatch(setNotification('An error occurred. Please try again.'));
        }
    }
};

With these changes, the backend will send a different error message when the password is too short, and the frontend will display the corresponding notification. The exception.response.status check ensures that you handle specific cases, and the generic error message serves as a fallback. Adjust the messages as needed.

You
does it matter if i use the word error or exception?
ChatGPT

In the context of programming, the terms "error" and "exception" are often used interchangeably, and there isn't a strict rule about which one to use. However, there are some common conventions:

  • "Error": This term is often used to refer to unexpected or exceptional situations that can occur during the execution of a program. It's a broad term that encompasses various issues, including both errors and exceptions.

  • "Exception": This term is typically used more specifically to refer to situations where something exceptional or unforeseen occurs during the program's execution. Exceptions are often associated with specific error types, such as syntax errors, runtime errors, or logical errors.

In your code, you can use either "error" or "exception" based on your preference or the convention followed in your codebase. The key is to use a consistent terminology within your code and be clear about the nature of the issues you are handling.

In the examples I provided earlier, I used "exception" in the variable names (exception), but you can easily replace it with "error" if you prefer that term. The important thing is to have clear and understandable variable names to make your code more readable for yourself and others.

You
what validations should i add for email address so that it must be valid email address?
ChatGPT

When validating an email address, you typically want to ensure that it adheres to a basic email format. While a complete validation of all possible email formats can be complex, a basic validation can be implemented using regular expressions. Here's a simple example in JavaScript:

javascript
const isValidEmail = (email) => {
  // Regular expression for a basic email format validation
  const emailRegex = /^[^\s@]+@[^\s@]+\.[^\s@]+$/;
  return emailRegex.test(email);
};

// Example usage
const email = "example@email.com";
if (isValidEmail(email)) {
  console.log("Valid email address");
} else {
  console.log("Invalid email address");
}

In this example, the isValidEmail function checks whether the provided email adheres to a basic email format. The regular expression used (/^[^\s@]+@[^\s@]+\.[^\s@]+$/) ensures that the email has a local part, an "@" symbol, a domain part, a dot, and a top-level domain.

Please note that while this basic validation can catch common mistakes, it won't guarantee that the email is deliverable or that it adheres to all aspects of the email specification (RFC 5322). Advanced email validation might involve more sophisticated checks and could include sending a confirmation email.

Also, keep in mind that client-side validation is helpful for providing immediate feedback to users, but server-side validation is crucial for security and should always be implemented to ensure data integrity.

You
add the regular expression to this code to check the email address similarly as the password length is checked: usersRouter.post('/', async (request, response) => { try { const body = request.body if (body.password.length < 6) { return response.status(400).json({ error: 'Password must be at least 6 characters!' }) } i const saltRounds = 10 const passwordHash = await bcrypt.hash(body.password, saltRounds) const user = new User({ name: body.name, username: body.username, passwordHash, email: body.email }) const savedUser = await user.save() response.status(201).json(savedUser) } catch (error) { console.error(error) response.status(400).json({ error: 'Please fill in all the fields to register!' }) } })
ChatGPT

You can add email validation to the existing code using a regular expression. Here's an updated version of your code that includes email validation:

javascript
const usersRouter = require('express').Router();
const bcrypt = require('bcrypt');
const User = require('../models/user');

usersRouter.post('/', async (request, response) => {
  try {
    const body = request.body;

    if (body.password.length < 6) {
      return response.status(400).json({ error: 'Password must be at least 6 characters!' });
    }

    // Regular expression for basic email format validation
    const emailRegex = /^[^\s@]+@[^\s@]+\.[^\s@]+$/;

    if (!emailRegex.test(body.email)) {
      return response.status(400).json({ error: 'Invalid email address!' });
    }

    const saltRounds = 10;
    const passwordHash = await bcrypt.hash(body.password, saltRounds);

    const user = new User({
      name: body.name,
      username: body.username,
      passwordHash,
      email: body.email,
    });

    const savedUser = await user.save();

    response.status(201).json(savedUser);
  } catch (error) {
    console.error(error);
    response.status(400).json({ error: 'Please fill in all the fields to register!' });
  }
});

module.exports = usersRouter;

In this version, a regular expression (emailRegex) is added to validate the email address format. If the email does not match the expected format, the server responds with a 400 status and an error message indicating an invalid email address.

You
is this enough to cause an error if the url alredy exists? or do i have to add checking of the url to pictures controller router? const pictureSchema = new mongoose.Schema({ url: { type: String, required: true, unique: true }, title: { type: String, required: true }, description: String, user: { type: mongoose.Schema.Types.ObjectId, ref: 'User' }, likedUsers : [ { type:mongoose.Schema.Types.ObjectId, ref: 'User' } ] })
ChatGPT

Yes, setting unique: true in the schema for the url field should be enough to ensure that each URL is unique within the collection. If you attempt to insert a document with a duplicate url, MongoDB will throw a duplicate key error, and it will not allow the insertion.

However, it's a good practice to handle this error in your application code and provide a user-friendly message or response. In your pictures controller router, you might want to catch the MongoDB duplicate key error and send a meaningful response to the client, indicating that the URL already exists.

Here's an example of how you might handle it:

javascript
const picturesRouter = require('express').Router();
const Picture = require('../models/picture');

picturesRouter.post('/', async (request, response) => {
  try {
    // Your existing code...

    const picture = new Picture({
      url: body.url,
      title: body.title,
      description: body.description,
      user: body.user,
    });

    const savedPicture = await picture.save();

    response.status(201).json(savedPicture);
  } catch (error) {
    if (error.name === 'MongoError' && error.code === 11000) {
      // Duplicate key error (URL already exists)
      response.status(400).json({ error: 'URL already exists.' });
    } else {
      // Other errors
      console.error(error);
      response.status(500).json({ error: 'Internal server error.' });
    }
  }
});

module.exports = picturesRouter;

In this example, if the error is a MongoDB duplicate key error (error code 11000), the server responds with a 400 status and an error message indicating that the URL already exists. For other errors, a generic internal server error message is sent.

You
is this correct: please fill in the pictures url address
ChatGPT

Your message "please fill in the pictures url address" is grammatically correct, but it might sound a bit more natural if you say "Please fill in the picture's URL address." This emphasizes that you are asking for the URL of a specific picture.

Alternatively, you could say "Please provide the URL for the picture." This is a slightly more concise way to convey the same message.

You
how to check if user has already liked the picture and send error if they have? usersRouter.post('/:id/likes', async (request, response) => { try { const userId = request.params.id const pictureId = request.body.pictureId const user = await User.findById(userId) if (!user.likedPictures.includes(pictureId)) { user.likedPictures.push(pictureId) await user.save() } response.status(200).json({ message: 'Picture liked!' }) } catch (error) { response.status(500).json({ error: 'Internal server error' }) } })
ChatGPT

If you want to check whether the user has already liked the picture and send an error if they have, you can modify your code to include this check. Here's an example:

javascript
usersRouter.post('/:id/likes', async (request, response) => {
    try {
        const userId = request.params.id;
        const pictureId = request.body.pictureId;
        const user = await User.findById(userId);

        if (user.likedPictures.includes(pictureId)) {
            return response.status(400).json({ error: 'User has already liked this picture.' });
        }

        user.likedPictures.push(pictureId);
        await user.save();

        response.status(200).json({ message: 'Picture liked!' });
    } catch (error) {
        response.status(500).json({ error: 'Internal server error' });
    }
});

In this modification, the code checks whether the likedPictures array of the user already includes the specified pictureId. If it does, the route returns a 400 status with an error message indicating that the user has already liked the picture. If not, the user's like is added, and a success message is returned.

You
is this good message to show user: Picture has already been saved to profile
ChatGPT

Yes, the message "Picture has already been saved to profile" effectively communicates to the user that the picture has already been added to their profile. It's clear and concise, providing the user with the information they need. If you believe this message conveys the intended information to your users, it should work well in your application.

You
how to write in commit message what i have today? i did those errors we have talked about and for example the email addres checking
ChatGPT

A good commit message typically follows the convention of summarizing the changes made in the commit. Here's an example of how you could write a commit message for the changes you made today:

diff
git commit -m "Fix errors and improve validation in registration process

- Ensure all required fields are filled during registration
- Add validation for password length (minimum 6 characters)
- Implement email address validation
- Address various issues discussed, improving overall application robustness"

In this example, each bullet point briefly describes a specific change or improvement made in the commit. Feel free to customize the message based on the actual changes you implemented. The goal is to provide a clear and concise summary of the modifications made in the commit.

You
can i say: add errors and validations
ChatGPT

While "add errors and validations" conveys a general sense of what you did, it's a bit too vague. A good commit message should be more specific about the nature of the errors or what validations were added. This specificity helps others (and your future self) understand the purpose of the changes without having to look into the details of the code.

Here's a more detailed example:

diff
git commit -m "Improve error handling and add validations in registration process

- Ensure all required fields are filled during registration
- Add validation for password length (minimum 6 characters)
- Implement email address validation
- Provide meaningful error messages for various scenarios"

In this message, the focus is on specific improvements related to error handling, validations, and the registration process. Adjust the message to accurately reflect the changes you made.

You
can i say: improve error handling and validations
ChatGPT

Yes, "Improve error handling and validations" is a concise and acceptable commit message. It provides a general overview of the changes you made without going into specific details, which is appropriate for a commit message. If you'd like to be more detailed, you can specify the areas where you improved error handling or the types of validations you added. However, the message you suggested is clear and informative.

You
synonym to improve
ChatGPT

A synonym for "improve" could be "enhance." So, an alternative commit message could be:

"Enhance error handling and validations."

You
what cyber security measures to take in to considerations when deploying webapp that is school project?
ChatGPT

When deploying a web application, even for a school project, it's important to consider cybersecurity measures to help protect the application and its users. Here are some key considerations:

  1. Secure Password Storage:

    • Use secure password hashing algorithms (e.g., bcrypt) to store user passwords.
    • Avoid storing plain-text passwords or using weak encryption methods.

  2. Data Encryption:

    • Use HTTPS to encrypt data transmitted between the user's browser and your web server.
    • Ensure sensitive data, such as user credentials, is encrypted both in transit and at rest.

  3. Authentication and Authorization:

    • Implement strong authentication mechanisms.
    • Use multi-factor authentication (MFA) if possible.
    • Enforce proper authorization controls to limit access to specific resources based on user roles.

  4. Session Management:

    • Implement secure session management techniques.
    • Use secure, random session identifiers.
    • Set session timeouts to automatically log users out after a period of inactivity.

  5. Input Validation:

    • Validate and sanitize all user inputs to prevent common security vulnerabilities like SQL injection and Cross-Site Scripting (XSS).

  6. Security Headers:

    • Use security headers, such as Content Security Policy (CSP), HTTP Strict Transport Security (HSTS), and X-Content-Type-Options, to enhance browser security.

  7. Error Handling:

    • Implement proper error handling to avoid exposing sensitive information in error messages.
    • Log errors securely and monitor logs for unusual activity.

  8. Regular Updates:

    • Keep all software components, including web servers, frameworks, libraries, and the operating system, up-to-date with the latest security patches.

  9. File Upload Security:

    • If your application allows file uploads, implement proper validation and restrictions to prevent malicious file uploads.

  10. Cross-Site Request Forgery (CSRF) Protection:

    • Implement CSRF tokens to protect against CSRF attacks.

  11. Security Headers:

    • Utilize security headers, such as Content Security Policy (CSP), to mitigate the risk of XSS attacks.

  12. Monitoring and Logging:

    • Set up monitoring for security events and regularly review logs for suspicious activity.

  13. Backup and Recovery:

    • Implement regular data backups and test the restoration process to ensure data recovery in case of a security incident.

  14. User Education:

    • Educate users about best practices for creating strong passwords and recognizing phishing attempts.

  15. Compliance:

    • Be aware of relevant data protection regulations (e.g., GDPR, HIPAA) and ensure compliance if applicable.

Remember that security is an ongoing process, and it's essential to stay informed about emerging threats and best practices in web application security.

You
what if the webapp is not going to be used?
ChatGPT

Even if a web application is not intended for widespread use or production deployment, it's still important to consider some cybersecurity measures to ensure the safety of the application and any data it may handle. Here are some key considerations, even for a non-production or limited-use web application:

  1. Secure Coding Practices:

    • Adhere to secure coding practices to minimize vulnerabilities in the application code.
    • Follow best practices for input validation, output encoding, and other secure coding principles.

  2. Authentication and Authorization:

    • Implement basic authentication mechanisms even if the application is used by a small group.
    • Enforce proper authorization controls to limit access to different parts of the application.

  3. Data Encryption:

    • Use HTTPS to encrypt data in transit, especially if the application deals with login credentials or sensitive information.

  4. Access Controls:

    • Restrict access to sensitive files, directories, or functions within the application.

  5. Error Handling:

    • Implement appropriate error handling to avoid information leakage.
    • Log errors securely for later analysis.

  6. Regular Updates:

    • Keep the application's dependencies, including libraries and frameworks, up-to-date with the latest security patches.

  7. Backup and Recovery:

    • Establish a backup strategy to prevent data loss in case of accidental deletion or system failures.

  8. Input Validation:

    • Validate and sanitize user inputs to prevent common vulnerabilities like SQL injection and Cross-Site Scripting (XSS).

  9. Monitoring:

    • Implement basic monitoring to detect unusual behavior or signs of potential security issues.

  10. Limited Exposure:

    • If the application doesn't need to be publicly accessible, limit its exposure by keeping it behind firewalls or on a local network.

  11. Educate Users:

    • If there are users interacting with the application, educate them about basic security practices and awareness.

  12. Disable Unused Features:

    • If the application has features that are not necessary for its limited use, consider disabling or removing them to reduce the attack surface.

While the level of security can be scaled based on the application's purpose and potential risks, incorporating these basic cybersecurity practices can help mitigate common vulnerabilities and ensure a baseline level of protection.

You
how to make home-button that is inside footer to be on the right side of the screen if other things in the footer are on the left side? .footer { width: 100; height: 15vh; padding: 10px; display: flex; margin-bottom: 5px; align-items: flex-start; } .home-button { align-items: left; margin: 5px; padding: 5px; border-radius: 10px; border: 1px solid rgba(5, 5, 5, 0.6); background-color: #f8e9f2; }
ChatGPT

To make the home button inside the footer be on the right side of the screen while other elements in the footer are on the left side, you can adjust the justify-content property of the .footer class. Here's an example:

css
.footer {
  width: 100%;
  height: 15vh;
  padding: 10px;
  display: flex;
  justify-content: space-between; /* This will push elements to the ends (left and right) */
  margin-bottom: 5px;
  align-items: flex-start;
}

.home-button {
  margin: 5px;
  padding: 5px;
  border-radius: 10px;
  border: 1px solid rgba(5, 5, 5, 0.6);
  background-color: #f8e9f2;
}

In this example, the justify-content: space-between; property for the .footer class will push its child elements to the left and right ends of the container, creating the desired effect. The home button will be on the right side, and other elements will be on the left side. Adjust the styling further if needed based on your design.

You
but i have two home-buttons and i want them both on the right side and other things on the left side
ChatGPT

If you have two home buttons and want both of them on the right side, you can create a separate container for the home buttons and set its alignment to the right. Here's an example:

css
.footer {
  width: 100%;
  height: 15vh;
  padding: 10px;
  display: flex;
  justify-content: space-between;
  margin-bottom: 5px;
  align-items: flex-start;
}

.home-buttons-container {
  display: flex;
  align-items: flex-start;
}

.home-button {
  margin: 5px;
  padding: 5px;
  border-radius: 10px;
  border: 1px solid rgba(5, 5, 5, 0.6);
  background-color: #f8e9f2;
}

/* Align home buttons to the right within their container */
.home-buttons-container {
  margin-left: auto;
}

In this example, the home-buttons-container is a separate container for the home buttons, and margin-left: auto; is used to align it to the right within the .footer. This way, the home buttons will be on the right side, and other elements will be on the left side. Adjust the styling based on your specific layout requirements.

You
edit this profile page form with css to look aesthetic and professional. <div className="profile"> <h2>{user.username}</h2> <p>Own pictures</p> <div className="layout"> {pictures.map((picture) => ( <div key={picture.id} className="picture"> <img src={picture.url} alt={picture.description} /> <button className="delete-button" onClick={() => handleDelete(picture.id)}>delete</button> </div> ))} </div> <p>Liked pictures</p> <div className="layout"> {likedPictures.map((picture) => ( <div key={picture.id} className="picture"> <img src={picture.url} alt={picture.description} /> <button className="delete-button" onClick={() => handleDeleteLike(user.id, picture.id)}>delete</button> </div> ))} </div> </div>
ChatGPT

Certainly! Here's an example of how you might style the profile page form to give it a more aesthetic and professional look. Feel free to adjust the styles based on your design preferences:

css
.profile {
  text-align: center;
  margin: 20px;
}

.profile h2 {
  font-size: 24px;
  margin-bottom: 10px;
}

.layout {
  display: flex;
  flex-wrap: wrap;
  justify-content: center;
  gap: 20px;
}

.picture {
  position: relative;
}

.picture img {
  max-width: 100%;
  border-radius: 10px;
  box-shadow: 0 4px 8px rgba(0, 0, 0, 0.1);
}

.delete-button {
  position: absolute;
  top: 10px;
  right: 10px;
  background-color: #ff6347; /* Coral color */
  color: #fff;
  padding: 5px 10px;
  border: none;
  border-radius: 5px;
  cursor: pointer;
}

.delete-button:hover {
  background-color: #d63447; /* Darker Coral on hover */
}

In this example:

  • The overall profile container is centered using text-align and given some margin.
  • Headings and buttons have specified font sizes and padding for better readability and interaction.
  • Pictures are displayed in a flex container, centered with some spacing between them.
  • The delete button is positioned at the top-right corner of each picture for a cleaner look.
  • A subtle box-shadow is added to the images for a slight elevation effect.
  • Colors are chosen for better contrast and aesthetics.

Feel free to adjust colors, fonts, and sizes to match your overall application design.

You
how to edit the profile form so that under user.username there is two buttons, "own pictures" and "saved pictures" and when you click own pictures, only users own pictures are shown. and when you click saved pictures, only likedPictures.map is showing
ChatGPT

To achieve this functionality, you can add state to track the active tab (Own Pictures or Saved Pictures) and then conditionally render the corresponding pictures based on the active tab. Here's an example of how you can modify your profile form:

Assuming you have a state variable like activeTab:

jsx
import { useState } from "react";

const Profile = ({ user, pictures, likedPictures, handleDelete, handleDeleteLike }) => {
  const [activeTab, setActiveTab] = useState("ownPictures");

  const handleTabChange = (tab) => {
    setActiveTab(tab);
  };

  return (
    <div className="profile">
      <h2>{user.username}</h2>
      
      <div className="tabs">
        <button onClick={() => handleTabChange("ownPictures")}>Own Pictures</button>
        <button onClick={() => handleTabChange("savedPictures")}>Saved Pictures</button>
      </div>

      <div className="layout">
        {(activeTab === "ownPictures" ? pictures : likedPictures).map((picture) => (
          <div key={picture.id} className="picture">
            <img src={picture.url} alt={picture.description} />
            <button
              className="delete-button"
              onClick={() => (activeTab === "ownPictures" ? handleDelete(picture.id) : handleDeleteLike(user.id, picture.id))}
            >
              Delete
            </button>
          </div>
        ))}
      </div>
    </div>
  );
};

In this example:

  • The activeTab state is used to keep track of the currently active tab.
  • Two buttons are added for "Own Pictures" and "Saved Pictures," and their click events update the activeTab.
  • The pictures displayed in the layout are conditionally rendered based on the activeTab.
  • The handleTabChange function updates the activeTab state when a button is clicked.

This way, when you click "Own Pictures," only the user's own pictures will be shown, and when you click "Saved Pictures," only the liked pictures will be displayed.

You
i have two of these buttons. make them be underlined and maybe something nice? .profile-button { background-color: white; border: 0px; margin-bottom: 20px; }
ChatGPT

To style your profile buttons with underlines and add some decorative elements, you can use CSS. Here's an example:

css
.profile-buttons {
  display: flex;
  justify-content: space-around;
  margin-bottom: 20px;
}

.profile-button {
  background-color: white;
  border: 0px;
  text-decoration: none; /* Remove default underline */
  position: relative;
  padding: 10px;
  color: #333; /* Text color */
}

.profile-button::after {
  content: '';
  display: block;
  position: absolute;
  bottom: 0;
  left: 50%;
  width: 0;
  height: 2px; /* Underline height */
  background-color: #007bff; /* Underline color */
  transition: width 0.3s ease; /* Transition for the underline effect */
}

.profile-button:hover::after {
  width: 100%;
  left: 0;
}

/* Additional styling for a nice hover effect */
.profile-button:hover {
  background-color: #f0f0f0;
  transition: background-color 0.3s ease;
}

Now, apply the profile-buttons class to the container of your profile buttons and the profile-button class to each individual button:

jsx
<div className="profile-buttons">
  <button className="profile-button" onClick={() => handleTabChange("ownPictures")}>Own Pictures</button>
  <button className="profile-button" onClick={() => handleTabChange("savedPictures")}>Saved Pictures</button>
</div>

This will create underlined profile buttons with a nice hover effect. Adjust the colors and styles as needed to fit your design preferences.

You
how to change button text color when hovering
ChatGPT

To change the button text color when hovering, you can use the :hover pseudo-class in your CSS. Here's an example:

css
.profile-button {
  background-color: white;
  border: 0px;
  text-decoration: none; /* Remove default underline */
  position: relative;
  padding: 10px;
  color: #333; /* Default text color */
  transition: color 0.3s ease; /* Transition for the text color change */
}

.profile-button:hover {
  color: #007bff; /* Text color on hover */
}

In this example, the color property is set to the default text color (#333), and when you hover over the button, it transitions to a new color (#007bff). Adjust the colors according to your design preferences.

You
is event.preventDefault() mandatory here, what does it do? const goToRegister = async (event) => { event.preventDefault() navigate(`/register`) }
ChatGPT

In the provided code snippet:

javascript
const goToRegister = async (event) => {
  event.preventDefault();
  navigate(`/register`);
}

event.preventDefault() is used to prevent the default behavior of an HTML form submission. When a form is submitted, the browser typically reloads the page or performs some default action. By calling event.preventDefault(), you're telling the browser not to execute its default behavior.

In this case, it's used in a function associated with a button or link (<a> element). If this function is called in response to a user click, without event.preventDefault(), the browser might try to navigate to the URL specified in the href or action attribute of the associated element. However, with event.preventDefault(), you're explicitly telling the browser not to navigate away, and instead, you're using the navigate function from React Router to handle the navigation within your React application.

So, in summary, it's mandatory here if you want to handle the navigation within your React application and prevent the default browser navigation behavior.

You
how to make this button be on the left side of the screen .home-button { margin: 5px; padding: 5px; border-radius: 10px; border: 1px solid rgba(5, 5, 5, 0.6); background-color: #f8e9f2; } .home-button:hover { background-color: rgb(245, 222, 236); }
ChatGPT

To make the .home-button appear on the left side of the screen, you can use the position property in CSS. Here's an example:

css
.home-button {
  position: fixed;
  left: 10px; /* Adjust the left value as needed */
  top: 10px; /* Adjust the top value as needed */
  margin: 5px;
  padding: 5px;
  border-radius: 10px;
  border: 1px solid rgba(5, 5, 5, 0.6);
  background-color: #f8e9f2;
}

.home-button:hover {
  background-color: rgb(245, 222, 236);
}

In this example, position: fixed is used to position the button relative to the browser window. The left and top properties are then used to specify the exact position from the left and top edges of the window. Adjust the left and top values according to your desired position.

You
picture-form and two home-buttons are inside footer. make so that picture-form is on the left side and home-buttons on the right side of the screen. .footer { width: 100; height: 15vh; padding: 10px; display: flex; margin-bottom: 5px; } .picture-form { justify-content: left; display: flex; height: 15vh; width: 30vh; align-items: center; text-align: center; font-size: smaller; border: 1px solid #e0e0e0; border-radius: 10px; box-shadow: 0 4px 8px rgba(0, 0, 0, 0.2); background-color: rgba(255, 255, 255, 0.6); color: rgba(5, 5, 5, 0.8); } .notification { position: fixed; top: 5%; right: 30%; background-color: #f5f5f5; width: 40%; border: 1px solid rgba(5, 5, 5, 0.6); border-radius: 10px; padding: 5px; text-align: center; z-index: 1000; } .home-button { position: fixed; right: 10px; top: 20px; margin: 5px; padding: 5px; border-radius: 10px; border: 1px solid rgba(5, 5, 5, 0.6); background-color: #f8e9f2; }
